feedback:
see my comments and our email

Dear [2 names of members of the survey project]!
 
Thank you very much for your email and query whether we will take your survey on Post-Publication Peer Review (PPPR),
 
We don’t want to step on your toes, but we are a bit surprised to see that your NanoBubbles EU project is presumably referring to and including comments from PubPeer in your investigation. But maybe we misunderstand? Please see the attached fresh article.
 
While there may be some relevant bits and pieces on the PubPeer platform our experience is solely negative. Basically, the platform is used to defame scientists with subjective non-scientific claims often based on AI-generated nonsense. Using it as a valid platform for serious post-publication discussions is like white-washing money. It is used for falsified claims to support secondary non-scientific agendas. Parallel to the comments on PubPeer, these are copy-pasted into harassing emails that are sent broadly to author colleagues, collaborators, journals where they are editors and even to the Rectorate of Universities including Ethics Departments. In our case all these has either been desk-rejected or post-investigation rejected. This creates a lot of work, unnecessary noise and defamation that pull the inertia out of scientific progress.
 
The overall problem with this is that people are anonymous using fake names and invented emails for the case, often stealing names of known persons. This is to hide their real identity including that they are not knowledgeable in the scientific field where they are anonymously chasing known scientists like us. The only one not being anonymous and clearly involved in all this is [sleuth name] and his group having a platform known as [name of platform] spreading lies and claims in a way so that he should be put into court.
 
In many cases their goal is for sure not to increase the quality of scientific purposes and for the greater good of our globe through PPPR, but to gain either monetary or kind of promotional advantages though we do not have any information about this. They could also basically be mental ill. As an example, one single profile that has also made claims towards us, has made more than 500 AI-generated claims on PubPeer on one single week. All very similar clearly trying to create a problem that is not there. Very sad to see. 
 
Our experience with PPPR conducted via PubPeer is that it’s used for non-scientific purposes and that we cannot support its use in any investigation given it’s not a controlled legal and scientific and ethically sound information platform with people of known identity and scientific background. Frankly, the quality and its aim are like the platforms X and Trump Truth. These are all equally used for invalid spread of lies, defamation, harassment and racist purposes used in a whole other contest. 
 
If you are interested, we can send some examples of our journal responses so that you can see what we are up against. These will of course be confidential; however, they will give you an idea of the problem. These are all accepted by the journals and the cases has been closed.
 
We wish you good luck with your project and we will take the survey now. 
 
We are sorry if some of the text above is a bit harsh, but we are getting very tired after 2 years of harassment.
 
Our best regards, [name], [name] and [name]
title: Final open comment — com52_5

feedback:
As has been studied and shown, the types of errors most often pointed out on pubpeer occur frequently. In manuscripts containing large numbers of (western blot (ours contained 87 panels), FACS, IHC,..) panels, duplication errors are quite common. These mistakes most often happen during labelling, figure assembly etc. 
I believe it is important to point out these mistakes and correct the scientific literature, to ensure veracity of the data and conclusions. Pubpeer and science journalism has played a big role in preventing author sloppiness, in better record keeping systems and in providing original data (now required for most open access journals). 
Having said that (and the reason I do not use pubpeer more myself), the platform has not evolved one step further toward discussion of the science, validity and reproducibility of conclusions in manuscripts (including those that do not contain panel duplications or splicing in western blots). The anomalies pointed out on pubpeer are not placed in context of the study as a whole or of the literature relevant to the study. In our case, the flagged panels confirmed data from an unbiased Mass Spec screen, and these data were further confirmed in the manuscript by functional data in multiple models. In fact the removal of the flagged panels would have had no effect on the conclusions of the manuscript, which were confirmed and published by multiple independent studies in the following years. 
In addition, given that many manuscripts contain the types of errors (which do not alter the conclusions of the manuscript), the specific selection of a minority of manuscripts to highlight can facilitate predatory anonymous finger pointing. Therefore the impact of the platform as a whole is limited from my perspective.

feedback:
Towards Ethical and Constructive Post-Publication Commentary in Science: The post-publication commentary is a critical element of scientific progress. If carried out responsibly, it serves to refine, validate, and sometimes correct published findings. However, in recent years, the misuse of anonymous online platforms for unverified and often non-expert criticism has raised serious ethical concerns. Instead of contributing to scholarly debate, these platforms are mostly weaponized for personal, institutional, or competitive motives, resulting in reputational harm and undue stress for researchers, and most concerning, damage to master's and PhD students' and postdocs' careers.

To maintain the integrity of the scientific process, post-publication commentary must adhere to the same standards of accountability and professionalism as peer review itself. This includes:

1) Transparency: Comments should be authored by individuals who disclose their identity and relevant expertise. Anonymity, while protecting whistleblowers in rare cases, should not be a default mode of communication in science.

2) Constructive Intent: The objective should be to clarify, question, or improve upon scientific work,  not to discredit or harass authors.

3) Evidence-Based Critique: Feedback must be supported by scientific reasoning, data, or clear methodological analysis, not opinions or speculative assumptions.

4) Proper Channels: Comments should be submitted to the journal editors, official post-publication peer review systems, or institutional forums designed to handle scientific discussions, not informal or social media platforms where moderation and expertise are limited.

5) Respect and Professionalism: Even when identifying flaws, the tone should remain respectful. Science thrives on debate, not defamation.

Establishing formal, moderated platforms for post-publication discussion, where contributors are verified and discussions are archived responsibly, is a more sustainable and ethical alternative. By doing so, we can preserve the credibility of science while encouraging openness, dialogue, and continuous improvement in published research.

feedback:
I believe that post-publication platforms such as PubPeer, which allow the unqualified posting of anonymous comments without any verification that they come from genuine scientists or represent constructive scientific critique, risk harming rather than advancing scientific rigor. While the integrity of science and published work is unquestionably critical, it is equally important to remember that there is a person on the receiving end of these comments. The unregulated ability for individuals to barrage authors with anonymous remarks can lead to significant personal harm—something such platforms appear to overlook.

It is essential to strike the right balance. Post-publication peer review is valuable when it fosters open scientific discourse and supports the improvement of research quality. However, it must not be weaponized to damage careers or reputations for personal gain. The purpose of such systems should be to protect the integrity of science while acknowledging that scientists, as individuals, are fallible. Many issues identified post-publication arise from genuine mistakes or differences in interpretation, and these should be addressed in a constructive, respectful manner.

Unfortunately, the tone on PubPeer often departs from constructive dialogue, with accusations that sometimes extend to questioning personal integrity. The veil of anonymity can embolden individuals to make unjustified claims without fear of direct consequences. For this reason, platforms of this nature should require registration with verified institutional or professional email addresses. This would ensure accountability and allow proportionate consequences, including bans, for those who make unfounded or malicious attacks.

feedback:
It is often the case these days that papers present significant volumes of data of diverse types, analysed using a range of approaches. The data may have been collected over many years by different people. It is not surprising if errors to creep in while collating everything for publication, however often the authors check and double check everything, and however careful the referees are. Correcting these errors post-publication is important, even if they do not impact on the conclusions drawn.

It is unfortunate that this whole endeavour has become so closely associated with concerns about fraud, and that the tone of some of those who help to police the literature has become so shrill. While identifying and addressing fraud is clearly essential, and there is a continuous gradient of issues from serious fraud through to minor inadvertent errors, it is particularly important that correcting inadvertent errors is seen as a normal part of the process of doing high quality research. The goal here is to maintain the robustness of the research record. This will be best achieved if making minor corrections is as positive an experience as possible for all those concerned.

feedback:
Our paper cites the paper in question once in the discussion, as one example of several to illustrate a point that is unaffected by the error(s) identified. Even if the cited paper had been completely retracted, which is not the case, it would have no impact on the conclusions of our paper and would not result in readers of our paper gaining any kind of inaccurate view of our results or the conclusions that can be drawn from them. Our paper is the closing off of one line of enquiry for us and we have not pursued further the results we reported. Therefore, there would be no benefit for others or for us in doing anything in response to the PubPeer comments.

My view is that the integrity of the published record is crucial and is best maintained if corrections are linked to the original paper and only propagated through the literature where there is a material impact. More extensive propagation of reporting on corrections would make it much harder to navigate the literature and therefore undermine the purpose of correction.

feedback:
I have many concerns regarding PubPeer as it is especially when it is used by the Journals to investigate on the works of authors that have been published more thatn 10 years ago. I do not question that it may be useful for starting a scientific discussion on a specific paper or part of it, I discuss the fact that there is no use to ask for original blots for papers published 10 or 15 years ago when sending original uncropped blots was not requested. How can the authors find those original blots if they were on films that are lost, ruined or else? Even stored scanned films cannot be found because computers get old and, for example, are discarded during laboratory renovations. I think it would be more useful to start a discussion with the authors involving others scientists and editors about their results and if these results have been confirmed by other groups or not. If this is the case then what is the point to ask for an actin because it looks similar to another actin of another blot? or question why the authors have used the same control in two different figurees?
It is correct that the journal are now asking the authors to submit original images of their work. What is not correct is to transform this to an obsession towards old published papers where it was not mandatory and when the journals had other rules. I am afraid that this is transforming into a witch hunt. I would find more useful if the journal check how many times the paper has been cited, how many of tese citations are reviewes and how many are original papers and if they reached the same conclusions of the paper under scrutiny or if they confirmed the results or not.
title: Explain PubPeer concern / citation context — cit194_1

feedback: The problem we are all facing with the roofs of our manuscript ist that one usually has a very short time slot (48 h or so), which is not sufficient to send it to all coauthors for approval. Moreover, the online proofs should only be used by one correcting person, since clicking a wrong button will submit the "final proof". Due to the online format it is impossible to send a pdf of the galley proof to the coauthors. Therefore, the corresponding author is often overexerted to check the proofs in such a short time. If you do not keep the deadline, the uncorrected proof will be published without any chance to correct it afterwards. Thus, the Jackass is not always on the side of the authors....

feedback: I do not feel that PubPeer provides a useful service. It is unclear if any of the people providing post-publication peer review have the requisite qualifications to identify true instances of fraud. The one instance that I was made aware of had no independent confirmation, and a comment on PubPeer does not rise to that level. the larger issue with peer review is the conditional nature of almost any bio,ogical experiment: It is far more likely that the specific growth conditions of a given publication are difficult to replicate in other labs, either through subtle differences in growth conditions that change cell behavior, differences in cell passage number, etc. In my opinion, these larger, but rather innocent differences maks whatever fraud is committed to cloud the conclusions we draw from science.

feedback: The comment on PubPeer refers to so-called “tortured phrases,” which we believe is a misunderstanding of standard academic writing practices. In scholarly work, the use of synonyms and varied terminology is not only common but encouraged, as it enhances clarity, avoids redundancy, and enriches the expression of ideas. There is no theoretical or methodological guideline that requires strict adherence to a single term when legitimate synonyms exist. The flagged phrases appear to be identified by automated tools that rely on usage patterns without understanding context or academic norms. Such tools often misclassify commonly used, well-established academic expressions as problematic. In fact, many of the terms flagged in our work have been in use for over a century and are found in peer-reviewed publications by leading academic publishers and in high-impact journals. The assumption that these terms are artificial constructs or the product of paraphrasing software misrepresents the nuanced and context-dependent nature of academic language. We strongly maintain that the terms in question are academically sound, appropriately used, and consistent with long-standing scholarly traditions. The critique based solely on automated detection tools fails to capture the legitimacy and contextual relevance of the language employed in our publication.

feedback: I believe it is important to add that pubpeer, while initially being a very good and valuable resource, turned very unjust and hatred-driven. Unfortunatelly, this beautiful idea was hijacked and corrupted into a hatred platform. No enthusiastic or supportive comments are published there anymore, no congratulations on beautiful results, no acknowledgements on hard and well-done work. Even an innocent mistake is blown out of proportion. It would be nice if the people who post their comments would sometimes say also something nice. For example: "we would like to bring your attention to the duplication of this image or mislabelling of this figure and would appreciate your attention to it. The rest of the manuscript (99%) is of high quality and we congratulate you on the good work..." Such nicely and humanly phrased comments are just a naive dream on pubpeer. In my experience and those of many others the accusatory and out-of-proportion comments are initiated because the author of the paper expressed a different opinion about certain societal or scientific issues. I publicly stood up for one of the scientist that in my opinion was falsely accused and asked for kindness, fairness and reserve until the matter is more justly approached. By the next day, anonymous pubpeer authors went through all my papers with an AI tools to pick any mistakes that might have been there. In my 20 years of service to science they found 2 innocent mistakes which were absolutely blown out of proportions. All of this because of their hunt to discredit me scientifically. And since they couldn't find anything that would discredit me they started commenting on many figures in my manuscript just asking seemingly innocent questions (why did you implement this tool and not that one...) all because they want to increase the number of comments under my papers. This in turn allows them to claim that "this author has 20 comments about his/her paper on pubpeer". This brings the assumption to the readers that they must be bad comments because everyone now knows that only bad comments and mistakes are being posted on pubpeer. And very few people actually go and check what those 20 comments are about. There is a nasty turn on pubpeer where this site was hijacked by so called "professional haters" that were hired by someone or are just spewing their anger on authors that happen to have an opinion they disagree with. None of the scientists around me is paying attention to pubpeer anymore, becasue of this very unbalanced, unhealthy reporting service. Pubpeer should delete all the comments written by unanimous people that hide themselves behind latin names. Only then it can make itself into a valid scientific platform.
title: Final open comment — com132_5

feedback:
I firmly believe it is inappropriate to undermine the extensive efforts of researchers, scientists, editors, and publishers by requiring formal responses to anonymous and unfounded comments on platforms such as PubPeer. Published papers undergo rigorous peer review, including blind evaluations, before being accepted by reputable journals. It is therefore unreasonable to expect authors and stakeholders to divert their time and attention to address baseless or illogical remarks from unidentified sources.

If such anonymous commentary is given undue weight, it risks shifting the focus of the academic community from advancing research to continuously defending it against unverified claims. One must also consider the broader implications: if similar platforms proliferate, will researchers and publishers be expected to respond to every speculative comment? Such a precedent could compromise the integrity and efficiency of scholarly communication.
title: Why not use PubPeer (other) — com86_2

feedback: The PubPeer comment addresses issues in writing, specifically those referred to as "tortured phrases." While these affect readability, they do not constitute a scientific error in the methods, results, or conclusions of the paper. Such language problems are common in publications authored by non-native English speakers and can sometimes pass through peer review without being corrected. In my view, these issues reflect challenges to scientific communication rather than flaws in the underlying research. Therefore, I would not classify them as scientific errors but rather as editorial shortcomings that should ideally have been addressed during the review and publication process.

feedback: I believe Post-Publication Peer Review to be a soapbox for malcontents.  I looked through my own publications that had been commented on in PubPeer after I got your email (4 of 110 had been listed) and the comments were not just untrue but spiteful and in no way changed the outcomes of the manuscripts.  Nor would any of the comments have elevate the standard of the science in the manuscript if we were to fix the indicated issues.  PubPeer, and Post-Publication Peer Review in general, is a playground full of anonomous bullies and keyboard assassins happy to pick at the loose threads of scientific research until then unravel.  Rarely does this process end with increased understanding of the topic matter or elevation of the science presented in the manscript and, as a consequence, is an exercise in "catching smoke".

feedback: Post-publication reviewing is mainly useful for uncovering errors, ethical breaches, and misconduct, but it has limited impact on scientific progress. Findings that cannot be reproduced are eventually disregarded, discredited, and no longer cited, while only robust results truly advance knowledge. Pre-publication peer review is thus essential as a first filter, even if imperfect. Reviewers, however, rarely have the expertise or tools to detect flaws in figures. This responsibility should fall on editors through systematic pre-publication checks. Moreover, scientists cannot realistically scrutinize PubPeer or similar platforms for every article they read, which often numbers in the hundreds during the preparation of a single manuscript. Post-publication review serves as an important corrective mechanism, but the foundation of progress remains the reliability ensured before publication.

It just seemed completely nitpicking and anachronistic, complaining about processes that were acceptable when the paper was originally published, and did not in any way change the scientific conclusion. Worse, the main point of the paper had actually been replicated by multiple later studies, so the issue raised was self-evidently not an error or a falsehood. In this case at least, this whole exercise just felt like typically academic navelgazing at its most egregious.

feedback: Reading through the comments, there is disagreement even among the peers about whether there is even an error or not.  In fact, it looks like the disagreement itself took on a life of its own.   If there were an error, it should have been addressed by/with the original authors, and the original publishers.   To my knowledge, neither a correction or any other comment has been provided by the original authors or publisher.   In my opinion, Pubpeer looks to be about the equivalent of X, where anyone can say just about anything.... it just happens to have science-oriented articles as the primary target.   There's only so much due diligence a busy, working scientist can perform and still get their work done.  The original publisher of the cited work is respected.  At some point folks have to take some things on faith if they still want to have any productivity.   The comment is 10 years old, and most journals these days already do image assessments of their own as part their own internal screening processes.  If it ever had a legitimate purpose, Pubpeer has long since outlived it any legitimacy it might have had.

feedback:
I find the PubPeer system problematic.  It provides a place where papers can be anonymously critiqued, and those critizing them can hide behind the anonymous nature of their critique.  In my view, it leads to a large amount of politically motivated and unsubstantiated comments that can have a large impact on the authors careers.  I am thankful that I have not yet had a paper on PubPeer, but I know of individuals whose careers got caught up in trying to refute comments that started on PubPeer.

I am not sure how to solve this problem, as I think it is important to preserve the capacity for anonymous critique, but some better policing of what is allowed to appear in public is required.

feedback:
I think the overall purpose and intention of pubpeer are positive in terms of academic development. In addition, I think the purpose of pubpeer should be to academically correct and improve errors or deficiencies in publications.

For the development of pubpeers, I would like to suggest the following improvement directions.

1) I think it is very inappropriate for readers who post their opinions on pubpeer to remain anonymous. It is not absolutely necessary for the questioner to be anonymous, and I think it should provide exactly the affiliation and career in the field of the paper in which he/she asked about.
2) Readers think it is very wrong to unilaterally claim their thoughts or guesses, especially to make claims that slander the author, and it does not fit the purpose of the pubpeer.
3) Readers' questions and authors' answers that were communicated in pubpeer should be conducted in an academic framework and should be improved with an academic manner and content.
Pubpeer does not have the purpose of being created and operated for slander, blame, and non-academic legal complaints/accusations between the reader and the author, does it?
4) Publishers and editors of each journal need to seriously consider the introduction of a formal and systematic process to re-evaluate and republish papers revised to academically agreed/derived content through questions and answers between readers and authors in pubpere.

I expect pubpeer to serve and develop not as a place of slander and criticism, but as a place where authors and works can develop through academic discussions.

feedback: With regard to PubPeer, I do not consider it a fully reliable source. While it can sometimes be a useful forum, there are significant concerns. Because comments are often anonymous, anyone can post remarks without accountability, and in some cases these comments are inaccurate or based on a superficial reading of the paper. For example, I personally received a comment that clearly demonstrated the commenter had not carefully read the paper or even the figure legend. Moreover, anonymity can enable targeting of individual researchers for non-scientific reasons, which undermines trust in the platform. In contrast to journal-based peer review, there is no formal process to check the validity or fairness of comments. For these reasons, I would only find PubPeer fully reliable if commenters were required to identify themselves (e.g., using institutional email addresses) and assume responsibility for their remarks.

feedback:
Concerns Regarding the Misuse of the PubPeer Platform: The online platforms like PubPeer were originally intended to foster post-publication discussion and enhance scientific accountability, their misuse has increasingly raised concerns within the academic community. In recent years, it has become evident that some individuals exploit such platforms not for constructive scientific critique, but rather to undermine the reputation of peer-reviewed publications and the researchers behind them.

Unfortunately, many comments on PubPeer come from anonymous or non-expert contributors, lacking verified technical expertise in the subject matter. This anonymity can lead to misleading interpretations and unfair damage to the reputations of authors whose work has already undergone rigorous peer review. In my opinion, expert evaluation is essential to scientific discourse, and a flood of unqualified or speculative comments can distort public understanding and create an unethical environment of distrust.

Moreover, it appears that in several cases, PubPeer and similar forums are used not for genuine scientific interest, but as a tool for professional rivalry or personal agendas. Without transparency about the qualifications or motivations of the commenter, it is impossible to assess the validity of the critique. Therefore, post-publication commentary should be approached with caution, and scientific critique should remain within the framework of responsible, verifiable, and peer-engaged discourse.

feedback: While I generally agree with the work and approaches of PubPeer, in this case, comments were made on old methodologies without the commenter understanding what was then considered standard practice. For example, northern blots were routinely stripped and re-used with new probes, and the original gel image was used for all of them in publications because they all derived from the same gel. I am not sure it was worth the time of the authors here to explain this to the commenter, nor the stress to the authors of having a paper flagged for possible fraud due to using once-common but now forgotten methodologies.

feedback: This was an attempt by the author of the PubPeer comment to produce a "gotcha" moment. title: Explain PubPeer concern / citation context — cit535_1

feedback: The nitpicking comments and gestapo-style inquisition of authors is the reason the general advice that I have heard from colleagues is to simply not engage. Scientists report that providing a more thorough explanation of a perceived discrepancy  is most often accompanied by renewed ferver in the inquisition. PubPeer should focus on identifying clear image duplication and manipulation with likely intent to commit fraud, and not on little inconsistencies like cropping and removing intervening gel lanes. Professional or hobbiest nitpickers that assume all authors are cheating is not a productive way to run post-publication peer review.
